teamObjective.explanation: Partner is down to 2 cards (near finish) while I hold only low non-trump cards. The best team outcome is to lead the weakest single so partner can win the trick, seize the lead, and likely go out.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner is likely trying to finish the hand soon and probably holds at least one card that can beat a low diamond-3, either a higher non-trump or a trump.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents likely hold enough mid/high non-trump cards or trumps to contest a low lead, but with no public plays yet their exact strength is uncertain; they will try to prevent partner from finishing.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the lowest single (diamond-3) maximizes the chance that partner can overtake and finish, and minimizes the chance that I win a trick I do not want to lead from again.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: A 4 is still low but slightly harder for partner to beat than a 3, and wastes a marginally stronger card when the objective is simply to surrender the lead.
A 5 increases the risk that opponents or partner let it ride and I retain the lead with weak remaining cards.
Leading the 6 is the strongest of my low cards; it is most likely to win the trick or force partner to use a higher card, neither of which helps my team.

risks: An opponent may beat the 3 and continue leading, delaying partner's finish.
Partner may not be able to beat the 3 if both remaining cards are lower non-trumps, causing the trick to be lost.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Leading the absolute lowest card minimizes the rank an opponent or partner needs to beat it, giving partner the best possible chance to take control while preserving my meager remaining hand.

notes: Hidden information is treated probabilistically; only public hand counts and the new-game event inform the beliefs.
